perturbation.original_text: All information is provided “as is” and without any warranty, express, implied, or otherwise, regarding its accuracy or performance.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the warranty disclaimer related to the accuracy of information introduces a legal risk. California law implies certain warranties, especially in business contexts. Removing the explicit disclaimer could make VMware liable for inaccurate information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Commercial Code § 2314 - Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade
perturbation.law_explanation: This section of the California Commercial Code implies a warranty of merchantability in contracts for the sale of goods, meaning the goods must be fit for the ordinary purposes for which they are used. By removing the disclaimer of warranty regarding the accuracy or performance of the information provided, the contract may unintentionally imply a warranty that the information is accurate and suitable for its intended purpose, potentially conflicting with California law if the information is later found to be inaccurate or unreliable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2314 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2314. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) Pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) In the case of fungible goods, are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) Are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) Run, within the variations permitted by the agreement, of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) Are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) Conform to the promises or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade
perturbation.justification: The original text included a disclaimer of warranty, which is a legal protection. By removing "express, implied, or otherwise, regarding its accuracy or performance" from the text, the modified text omits a legal protection related to warranty disclaimers. Scraped Law Snippet 1, COM 2314, states that a warranty of merchantability is implied in contracts for the sale of goods unless it is excluded or modified according to Section 2316. The omission of the warranty disclaimer could lead to an implied warranty and potential legal liability, creating a legal contradiction as the omission violates consumer protection.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees to comply with all United States export controls with respect to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees to comply with export controls with respect to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'United States' specification from export control compliance creates ambiguity. While it might seem minor, it could be interpreted as requiring compliance with any country's export controls, which is vague and potentially unenforceable under US law, and could lead to the recipient unintentionally violating US Export Control laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR, specifically 15 CFR § 732.3, outlines the steps for determining export control requirements under US law. By omitting the reference to 'United States' export controls, the modified text introduces uncertainty about which export control regime applies, creating potential conflict with US export regulations that companies are legally obligated to follow. This ambiguity could lead to unintentional violations of the EAR.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the 'United States' specification, creating ambiguity regarding which export control regime applies. The Law Explanation highlights that the EAR, specifically 15 CFR § 732.3, outlines export control requirements under US law. By omitting 'United States,' the contract introduces uncertainty, potentially conflicting with US export regulations, as the recipient might misinterpret or unintentionally violate them. Scraped Law Snippet 2 emphasizes that the EAR governs export regulations, underscoring the importance of specifying 'United States' to avoid ambiguity and ensure compliance with US law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreem ent shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of California as they apply to contracts entered into and wholly to be performed in the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'as they apply to contracts entered into and wholly to be performed in the State of California', the governing law clause becomes overly broad. Not all California laws apply to all contracts; the restriction ensures only relevant contract law applies. This omission creates uncertainty and could lead to misapplication of laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Civil Code § 1646 - Interpretation of Contracts
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1646 states that a contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed, or, if it indicates a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. By omitting the clause that restricts application of California law to contracts wholly performed within the state, the contract creates the potential for misinterpretation and conflict with this code section, especially if the agreement has connections to other jurisdictions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1646 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract text limits the governing law to California laws applicable to contracts 'entered into and wholly to be performed in the State of California.' The changed text broadens this to 'the laws of the State of California,' potentially conflicting with California Civil Code § 1646. The scraped law snippet states that a contract should be interpreted according to the law of the place of performance, or if no place is indicated, the place where it was made. By removing the restriction related to performance within California, the modified text opens the door for misinterpretation and application of laws not relevant to the contract's actual place of performance, directly contradicting the principle outlined in § 1646.

perturbation.original_text: The federal and state courts within the State of Calif ornia shall have exclusive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The courts within the State of California shall have jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'exclusive jurisdiction' to just 'jurisdiction' removes the guarantee that disputes will be settled *only* in California courts. This opens the door for disputes to be litigated in other jurisdictions, which could contradict California law if the agreement is designed to be interpreted solely under California law, and conflicts arise where laws diverge.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure § 410.30 - Forum Non Conveniens
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 410.30 addresses the doctrine of forum non conveniens, allowing a court to decline to exercise jurisdiction if there is a more convenient forum elsewhere. By removing the 'exclusive' nature of the jurisdiction clause, the modified text allows for the possibility that a court in California could defer to another jurisdiction, potentially undermining the intent to have disputes resolved under California law, and creating a conflict with the principles intended by having an exclusive jurisdiction clause.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 410.30 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.30. (a) When a court upon motion of a party or its own motion finds that in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state, the court shall stay or dismiss the action in whole or in part on any conditions that may be just. (b) The provisions of Section 418.10 do not apply to a motion to stay or dismiss the action by a defendant who has made a general appearance. (Amended by Stats. 1972, Ch. 601. Note: This version was suspended from Sept. 22, 1986, until Jan. 1, 1992, during operation of the temporary amendment by Stats. 1986, Ch. 968.)
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'exclusive jurisdiction' in California courts, while the changed text only mentions 'jurisdiction,' omitting the exclusivity. This omission potentially allows for disputes to be heard outside of California, which could conflict with California law if the agreement is intended to be interpreted solely under California law. CCP 410.30(a) states that a court may stay or dismiss an action if 'in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state'. The removal of 'exclusive' opens the door for the application of 410.30(a), potentially undermining the original intent of exclusive California jurisdiction, creating a legally significant contradiction.
